source.title: Caravans to Cathay
source.summary: In 1324, Mansa Musa of Mali crossed the Sahara with 60,000 people and so much gold that he crashed the precious metals market in Cairo.
source.author: Islamic Economics
source.body_text: In 1324, Mansa Musa of Mali crossed the Sahara with 60,000 people and so much gold that he crashed the precious metals market in Cairo. His journey was just one moment in a much larger story: the greatest pre-modern trading bloc in history. Journey across the Silk Road, the Indian Ocean dhow routes, and the trans-Saharan gold trade with Ibn Battuta as our guide. Topics: Silk Road, Indian Ocean Trade, Mansa Musa, Ibn Battuta, Trans-Saharan Trade, Amana (Trust) Series: Historical Islamic Economics (Part I) • Episode 5 of 6

x.single_post: Most people think ancient trade was about moving goods. It was actually about moving trust across impossible distances.
thread: Most people think ancient trade was about moving goods. It was actually about moving trust across impossible distances.
In 1324, Mansa Musa crossed the Sahara with 60,000 people and so much gold he crashed Cairo's precious metals market.
His journey was one thread in the largest pre-modern trading network ever built. The Silk Road, Indian Ocean routes, trans-Saharan gold trade.

Ibn Battuta traveled this world for thirty years. What he found wasn't just commerce—it was a system of trust that spanned three continents. Listen: https://islamiceconomics.github.io/podcast.html#ep-5

linkedin.post: In 1324, when Mansa Musa crossed the Sahara with 60,000 people and enough gold to crash Cairo's precious metals market, he wasn't just making a pilgrimage. He was participating in the greatest pre-modern trading network in history. The medieval Islamic world created something remarkable: a system of trust that connected the Silk Road, Indian Ocean dhow routes, and trans-Saharan gold trade into one vast economic web. Merchants could trade across three continents based on contracts, reputation, and shared commercial law. Ibn Battuta spent thirty years traveling these routes, witnessing how trust—not just goods—moved across impossible distances. This wasn't primitive barter. It was sophisticated finance built on the foundation of amana: sacred trust in commercial relationships. Listen: https://islamiceconomics.github.io/podcast.html#ep-5
linkedin.comment_prompt: What parallels do you see between medieval Islamic trade networks and modern global commerce?

instagram.caption: In 1324, Mansa Musa of Mali crossed the Sahara with 60,000 people and so much gold that he crashed the precious metals market in Cairo. 🏜️✨ But his journey was just one thread in an incredible story: the greatest pre-modern trading network ever built. The medieval Islamic world connected the Silk Road, Indian Ocean dhow routes, and trans-Saharan gold trade into one vast economic web. Ibn Battuta traveled these routes for thirty years, witnessing how trust—amana—moved across three continents. Merchants could trade from Spain to Indonesia based on reputation, contracts, and shared commercial law. This wasn't primitive exchange. It was sophisticated global finance, built on the sacred foundation of trust in commercial relationships. Listen to the full story: https://islamiceconomics.github.io/podcast.html#ep-5 #IslamicEconomics #MansaMusa #SilkRoad #IbnBattuta #MedievalTrade

upscrolled.post:
In 1324, Mansa Musa crossed the Sahara with so much gold he crashed Cairo's precious metals market. His journey revealed something extraordinary: the medieval Islamic world had built the greatest pre-modern trading network in history.

Ibn Battuta spent thirty years traveling these routes—from the Silk Road to the Indian Ocean to trans-Saharan gold trade. What he found wasn't just commerce, but a system of trust that spanned three continents.
upscrolled.discussion_prompt: How did medieval Islamic merchants build trust across such vast distances without modern communication?
upscrolled.topic_suggestion: Amana: Sacred trust in Islamic commerce

short_video.title: When Mansa Musa's Gold Crashed the Market
short_video.hook: In 1324, one man's pilgrimage crashed an entire economy
short_video.voiceover: In 1324, Mansa Musa of Mali crossed the Sahara with sixty thousand people and so much gold that he crashed Cairo's precious metals market. His journey was just one thread in the greatest pre-modern trading network ever built. The medieval Islamic world connected the Silk Road, Indian Ocean routes, and trans-Saharan trade into one vast economic web. Ibn Battuta traveled these routes for thirty years, witnessing how trust—not just goods—moved across three continents. This wasn't primitive exchange. It was sophisticated global finance built on sacred commercial trust.
